title: How to Deal with Spiritual Confusion About Your Gifts
meta_description: Stop questioning your sanity. Your experiences are real. This article cuts through the noise to help you trust your gifts and end the confusion for good.
og_title: How to Deal with Spiritual Confusion About Your Gifts
og_description: Stop questioning your sanity. Your experiences are real. This article cuts through the noise to help you trust your gifts and end the confusion for good.
body:
You're Not Broken. You're Just Confused. Here's the Real Talk.

Let me guess. You've had experiences that don't fit the normal script. You knew who was calling before you picked up the phone. You walked into a room and felt the mood shift like a physical weight. You've had dreams that came true, or you've felt someone's pain in your own body. And when you tried to talk about it, you got the look. The "you're being dramatic" look. The "maybe you should see someone" look. So you stopped talking. You started wondering if something's genuinely wrong with you.

Nothing's wrong with you. You're not broken. You're not making this up. Your nervous system is just calibrated for a wider range of input than most people's. That's not a defect. It's a design feature that nobody gave you the manual for.

Here's the thing nobody tells you: spiritual confusion about your gifts is not a sign that you're doing it wrong. It's a sign that you're actually doing it. Real intuitive development doesn't come with a clear path. It comes with a lot of "wait, what?" moments. And that's normal. That's the territory. Let's talk about what's actually happening and how to stop spinning your wheels.

"The confusion isn't a sign that you're lost. It's a sign that you're leaving the map behind. And that's exactly where you need to be."

Why You Feel Like You're Losing Your Mind (Spoiler: You're Not)

First, let's name the elephant in the room. Spiritual confusion feels awful. It feels like you're standing in a fog with no landmarks. You don't know what's real and what's your imagination. You don't know if you should trust that gut feeling or ignore it. You don't know if you're picking up on something genuine or just projecting your own anxiety.

I've been there. I know what it's like to lie awake at 3 AM wondering if you're psychic or just anxious. I know the shame of second-guessing every intuitive hit. And I know the exhaustion of trying to figure out who you can safely talk to about this stuff without getting labeled as weird or unstable.

Here's what's actually happening: your intuitive senses are waking up, but your rational mind doesn't have a approach for them yet. It's like suddenly being able to hear a radio station that nobody else can tune into. You're not crazy. You're just picking up a signal that most people's radios aren't set to receive. The confusion comes from not knowing what to do with that signal. Does that land?

The Three Types of Spiritual Confusion (And Which One You're Dealing With)

Not all confusion is the same. Once you can name what kind you're experiencing, it gets a lot easier to work through. Here are the three main types I see in people who are waking up:

1. The "Is This Real?" Confusion. This is the most common. You get a hit of intuition - a feeling, a knowing, a flash of imagery - and immediately your rational mind jumps in with "that's just your imagination." You start questioning everything. Was that a real psychic impression or just wishful thinking? The answer is usually both. Your intuition speaks through imagination. It's not separate from it. The key is learning to distinguish between your creative mind running wild and your intuitive mind delivering information. That takes practice. It takes making mistakes. It takes learning to trust the signal even when it's fuzzy.

2. The "What Do I Do With This?" Confusion. You get clear information. You know something. But you have no idea what to do with it. Maybe you sense that a friend is struggling, but you don't know if you should say something. Maybe you get a vision about a future event, but you don't know if you're supposed to act on it or just observe. This confusion comes from not having a clear relationship with your gifts. You're receiving information without a approach for how to use it. That's fixable.

3. The "Who Am I Now?" Confusion. This is the deep one. When your intuitive gifts start opening, your whole identity shifts. The person you thought you were - the practical one, the logical one, the one who doesn't believe in that stuff - starts to feel like a costume you've been wearing. You don't know who you are without it. This is the most disorienting kind of confusion, and it's also the most important. It's the sign that you're shedding an old identity to make room for a truer one.

Stop Looking for Certainty. Start Looking for Clarity.

Here's the hard truth that nobody wants to hear: you're never going to get absolute certainty about your gifts. Not in the way your rational mind wants certainty. Your intuitive abilities don't come with a guarantee. They come with a feeling. A knowing. A sense that something is true even if you can't prove it.

The mistake most people make is trying to force their intuitive experiences into a box of logical proof. They want to be 100% sure that the feeling they got was real before they act on it. And that's how you stay stuck in confusion forever. Because intuition doesn't work that way. It works on probability. On resonance. On a sense of "this feels right" even when you can't explain why.

What you want is not certainty. What you want is clarity. Clarity is knowing what you're feeling and why, even if you don't have all the answers. Clarity is being able to say "I'm picking up something here, and I'm going to hold it lightly and see what develops" instead of "I need to know right now if this is real or not."

You can develop that clarity. It's a skill. And like any skill, it takes practice. You don't learn to trust your intuition by thinking about it. You learn by using it. By making small bets on it. By noticing when you were right and when you were wrong. By building a track record of experience that slowly replaces confusion with confidence.

A Simple Practice to Cut Through the Noise

When you're in the thick of confusion, here's something you can do right now. Sit down with a journal. Write down the question that's bothering you. Something like "Is this intuitive hit real?" or "Should I trust what I'm feeling?"

Then, instead of trying to answer the question from your head, drop into your body. Take three slow breaths. Feel your feet on the floor. Notice the weight of your body in the chair. Then ask the question again, but this time pay attention to how your body responds. Does your chest tighten? Does your stomach relax? Do you feel a sense of expansion or contraction?

Your body knows things your mind doesn't. Your nervous system processes information way faster than your conscious thoughts can. When you're confused, you're usually stuck in your head, spinning in circles. Your body can give you a direct read on what's true for you right now. It's not about getting the "right" answer. It's about getting an answer that feels aligned with your whole being, not just your overthinking mind.

Try this for a week. Every time you feel confused about your gifts, stop and check in with your body. Write down what you notice. You'll start to see patterns. You'll start to recognize the physical signature of real intuition versus anxious imagination. That's how you build trust. That's how you move from confusion to clarity.

Why Other People's Opinions Are Making It Worse

Let's talk about the elephant in the room that nobody wants to name: the people around you are probably making your confusion worse. Not because they're bad people. Because they don't have the same wiring you do. They're operating on a different frequency, and when you try to explain your experiences to them, you end up feeling like you're speaking a language they don't understand.

Here's the thing: you don't need everyone to understand your gifts. You need a few people who get it. You need a teacher, a mentor, or a community of people who are also waking up. Trying to get validation from people who aren't on this path is like trying to get a fish to validate your experience of climbing a tree. It's not going to work. And it's going to leave you feeling confused and alone.

One of the best things you can do for your spiritual confusion is to stop looking for confirmation from people who can't give it. Start looking for people who are where you want to be. People who have been through the confusion and come out the other side. People who can say "Yeah, I went through that too. Here's what helped me."

If you're looking for a place to start, there are books that can act as that mentor until you find a live one. See on Amazon (paid link) is a solid resource for understanding the early stages of intuitive awakening. It doesn't sugarcoat the confusion. It meets you where you are.

The Trap of "Spiritual Bypassing" (And How to Avoid It)

One of the sneakiest forms of spiritual confusion is what's called spiritual bypassing. That's when you use spiritual concepts to avoid dealing with real emotions. You tell yourself "everything happens for a reason" when you're actually just avoiding grief. You say "I'm just not meant to know right now" when you're actually afraid of what you might discover.

Spiritual bypassing feels like clarity, but it's actually a way of staying confused without having to face it. It's a defense mechanism dressed up in spiritual language. And it will keep you stuck for years if you let it.

Real spiritual development doesn't bypass your humanity. It goes through it. You don't transcend your confusion by pretending it's not there. You work through it. You feel the discomfort. You ask the hard questions. You sit with the not-knowing until something shifts.

If you notice yourself using spiritual language to avoid feeling your feelings, that's a red flag. Stop. Get real with yourself. What are you actually feeling? What are you actually afraid of? The truth will set you free, but first it'll piss you off. That's okay. That's part of the process.

How to Know If You're Making Progress (When Everything Still Feels Confusing)

Here's a question I get all the time: "How do I know if I'm making progress if I still feel confused?" It's a fair question. And the answer might surprise you.

Progress in intuitive development doesn't look like less confusion. It looks like a different quality of confusion. Early confusion is panicked confusion. It's "Oh my god, I'm losing my mind, what's happening to me?" Later confusion is curious confusion. It's "Huh, that's interesting. I don't know what that means yet, but I'm willing to find out."

If you're still confused but you're not panicked about it, you're making progress. If you can sit with a question and not need an immediate answer, you're making progress. If you can have an intuitive experience and not immediately invalidate it, you're making progress.

The goal is not to eliminate confusion. The goal is to develop a relationship with it. To learn to hold it lightly. To trust that you don't need to have all the answers right now. To know that confusion is just the space between what you used to believe and what you're about to understand.

Another great resource for this phase is See on Amazon (paid link). It's written for people who are deep in the messy middle of awakening and need a grounded, no-bullshit guide to navigating the confusion without losing their footing.

Three Signs You're Actually Healing (Even If It Doesn't Feel Like It)

Let me give you three concrete signs that you're moving through the confusion, not just stuck in it:

1. You're less reactive. When someone questions your experiences, you don't feel the need to defend or explain. You just know what you know, and you're okay with them not getting it. That's a sign of real confidence, even if it's quiet confidence.

2. You're more curious. Instead of needing answers, you're genuinely interested in the questions. You can explore your experiences without needing to label them as "real" or "not real" right away. You're willing to hold space for the mystery.

3. You're more grounded. Your spiritual experiences don't send you spinning. You can have a powerful intuitive hit and still function in the real world. You're not getting lost in the spiritual realm. You're learning to integrate it with your daily life.

If you see these signs in yourself, even a little bit, you're on the right track. Keep going. The confusion is not your enemy. It's the chrysalis. You're in the process of becoming something new, and that process is messy by design.

The One Thing That Changes Everything

I've been doing this work for a long time. I've worked with hundreds of people who were drowning in spiritual confusion. And there's one thing that consistently makes the difference between people who stay stuck and people who break through: they stop trying to figure it out alone.

You were never meant to work through this alone. Your gifts are relational. They come alive in connection. When you try to figure everything out in isolation, you end up in a feedback loop of your own doubts. You need mirrors. You need people who can reflect back what they see in you. You need someone who can say "I see that gift in you, and here's how you can work with it."

That's why finding a teacher or a community is so critical. It's not about someone giving you all the answers. It's about having a container where you can explore your experiences without judgment. Where you can say "I think I picked up on something" and have someone say "Tell me more about that" instead of "Prove it."

If you're ready to stop spinning and start grounding, See on Amazon (paid link) is another excellent companion for this stage. It's practical, direct, and gives you exercises you can actually do instead of just concepts to think about.

Final Thoughts (No Fluff, Just Real Talk)

Here's where I land on all of this. Your spiritual confusion is not a problem to be solved. It's a process to be lived. You don't get to the other side by eliminating confusion. You get there by learning to move through it with grace, curiosity, and a whole lot of self-compassion.

You're not broken. You're not doing it wrong. You're waking up. And waking up is disorienting. It's supposed to be. The confusion is the sign that the old structures are falling away and something new is being built. Trust the process. Trust yourself. And for the love of all things, stop trying to do this alone.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work and get some real, practical guidance for navigating your unique gifts, I've put together a short assessment that can help you identify where you are in your awakening and what you need most right now. Take the free assessment here or check out the quick quiz to get some clarity on your next step.
